docs.title: Google ADK — GovernancePlugin Proposal
docs.text: Submission: google/adk-python#4543 Status: ✅ Implemented — ADK GovernanceAdapter shipped (agent-governance-python/agentmesh-integrations/adk-agentmesh/). PolicyEvaluator aligned with ADK protocol. Issue #4543 open on google/adk-python. Type: Feature request (BasePlugin implementation) Date Submitted: March 2, 2026

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Proposal to contribute a GovernancePlugin for Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK) that provides policy-based access control, threat detection, and audit trails. The plugin leverages ADK's existing BasePlugin hook architecture — no framework changes needed.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
ADK's plugin architecture (BasePlugin) has all the right hooks for governance enforcement — before_tool_callback, before_agent_callback, on_user_message_callback — but there's no built-in governance plugin. The existing plugins cover analytics (BigQuery), logging, context filtering, and retry, but nothing for policy-based access control, threat detection, or audit trails.

Enterprise teams building multi-agent systems need to enforce who can call what tools, detect dangerous prompts before they reach agents, and maintain compliance-grade audit logs.

docs.title: Proposed GovernancePlugin
docs.text: A BasePlugin implementation providing four capabilities:

docs.title: 1. Tool-Level Policy Enforcement (before_tool_callback)
docs.text:
• Allowlist/blocklist tools per policy
  • Block on content patterns (credentials, PII)
  • Enforce rate limits per agent/tool combination

docs.title: 2. Prompt Threat Detection (on_user_message_callback)
docs.text:
• Scan user messages for data exfiltration signals
  • Detect privilege escalation attempts
  • Identify prompt injection patterns
  • Block system destruction signals
  • All scanning happens before messages reach the agent

docs.title: 3. Agent-Level Trust Gating (before_agent_callback)
docs.text:
• Verify trust scores before allowing agent delegation
  • Enforce trust thresholds in multi-agent systems
  • DID-based identity verification (optional)

docs.title: 4. Audit Trail (after_tool_callback + after_agent_callback)
docs.text:
• Append-only log of all governance decisions
  • SHA-256 hash chain for tamper evidence
  • JSON Lines format for log aggregation compatibility

docs.title: Example API
docs.text:
from google.adk.runners import Runner
from governance_plugin import GovernancePlugin, GovernancePolicy

policy = GovernancePolicy(
    name="production",
    allowed_tools=["search_docs", "query_db", "create_ticket"],
    blocked_tools=["shell_exec", "delete_records"],
    blocked_patterns=[r"(?i)(api[_-]?key|password)\s*[:=]"],
    max_calls_per_request=25,
    require_human_approval=["create_ticket"],
)

runner = Runner(
    agent=root_agent,
    plugins=[GovernancePlugin(policy=policy)],
)

docs.title: Design Decisions
docs.text: Decision Approach Rationale Policy source YAML/JSON config files Policies change without deploys Composition Most-restrictive-wins merging Org → Team → Agent layering Fail mode Closed (deny on error) Safety-first for production Audit format JSON Lines Compatible with log aggregation Threat detection Regex pattern matching Deterministic, auditable, no LLM dependency

docs.title: Why Not Existing Samples?
docs.text: Existing Limitation safety-plugins Focuses on Google Model Armor (cloud-dependent content safety) policy-as-code Focuses on infrastructure policy checking (Terraform/OPA) This proposal Runtime tool-level governance — controlling what agents can do within execution, independent of cloud services

docs.title: Cross-Framework Validation
docs.text:
This pattern has been validated across multiple frameworks:

Framework Package Tests PydanticAI pydantic-ai-governance 57 CrewAI crewai-agentmesh — Microsoft Agent Framework MAF middleware adapter 18 Mastra @agentmesh/mastra 19 Agent OS (core) agent-os 1,327

docs.title: OWASP Coverage
docs.text:
The GovernancePlugin covers 10/10 OWASP Agentic Top 10 risks through ADK's native hooks:

  • before_tool_callback → ASI-01 (Hijacking), ASI-02 (Excessive Capabilities), ASI-06 (Confused Deputy)
  • on_user_message_callback → ASI-01 (Hijacking), ASI-05 (Insecure Output)
  • before_agent_callback → ASI-03 (Insecure Communication), ASI-07 (Identity Spoofing)
  • after_tool_callback → ASI-09 (Missing Audit Trails)

docs.text:
• Package: agent-governance-python/agentmesh-integrations/haystack-agentmesh/

docs.title: Components
docs.text: Component File Description GovernancePolicyChecker governance.py Enforces governance policies on agent actions — tool allowlist/blocklist, content pattern filtering (substring, regex, glob), token limit enforcement, per-agent rate limiting with time-based windows. Outputs decision (allow/deny/audit), reason, and passed flag. TrustGate trust_gate.py Evaluates agent trust scores (0.0–1.0) and routes decisions based on configurable thresholds. Tracks success/failure with reward/penalty adjustments and time-based trust decay. Outputs trusted flag, score, and action (pass/review/block). AuditLogger audit.py Append-only tamper-evident audit logging with SHA-256 hash chain integrity. Each entry hashes to the previous for immutability verification. Supports JSONL export for offline analysis. Outputs entry_id and chain_hash.

docs.title: Internal Data Classes
docs.text: Class File Description AgentTrustRecord trust_gate.py Internal state container for per-agent trust data (score, successes, failures, last_update) AuditEntry audit.py Immutable audit record container (entry_id, timestamp, action, agent_id, decision, metadata, prev_hash, chain_hash)

docs.title: Graceful Fallback
docs.text:
All three components implement identical fallback logic when haystack-ai is not installed:

try:
    from haystack import component
except ImportError:  # pragma: no cover
    class _ComponentShim:
        def __call__(self, cls):
            return cls
        @staticmethod
        def input_types(**kwargs):
            def decorator(func): return func
            return decorator
        @staticmethod
        def output_types(**kwargs):
            def decorator(func): return func
            return decorator
    component = _ComponentShim()

This ensures components function as regular Python classes even without Haystack installed, while registering as proper SuperComponents when Haystack is available.

docs.title: Hyperlight Sandbox Isolation Design
docs.text: Field Value Status Draft Author Amol Ravande Reviewer AGT Core Team Date 2026-05-06 Package agent-sandbox Upstream hyperlight-sandbox v0.4.0 (CNCF Sandbox)

docs.title: Motivation
docs.text:
DockerSandboxProvider gives agent-sandbox a hardened container backend, but container isolation is fundamentally kernel-shared. A kernel exploit, a container-escape CVE, or a misconfigured runtime breaks the boundary. For high-risk (Ring 3) agents — third-party plugins, marketplace agents, and code generated by an untrusted upstream — we need a sandbox where the guest cannot reach the host kernel at all.

Hyperlight is a lightweight Virtual Machine Manager (VMM) that runs guest code in micro virtual machines with hardware-level isolation via KVM (Linux), Microsoft Hypervisor (mshv on Azure Linux), or Windows Hypervisor Platform (WHP on Windows 11 / Server 2022+). Each guest runs without a general-purpose OS and without a filesystem.

hyperlight-sandbox is a separate CNCF Sandbox project (Apache-2.0) that wraps Hyperlight with a unified, multi-backend sandboxing API and ships official Python and .NET SDKs. It provides everything HyperLightSandboxProvider needs out of the box:

  • a Python SDK (pip install "hyperlight-sandbox[wasm,python_guest]"),
  • a packaged Python guest that runs real Python source inside a Wasm component on hyperlight-wasm (no custom guest binary required),
  • register_tool(name, callable) for tool dispatch with schema-validated args,
  • allow_domain(url) plus a built-in http_get / fetch exposed to the guest only after a domain is allowed,
  • snapshot() / restore(snap) for in-memory rewind, and
  • read-only /input and writable /output capability-based file access.

The right move for AGT is to adopt hyperlight-sandbox as the underlying runtime and write HyperLightSandboxProvider as a thin adapter from the SandboxProvider ABC defined in DOCKER-SANDBOX-ISOLATION-DESIGN.md. Rebuilding what upstream already ships would duplicate effort and fall behind on security fixes.

HyperLightSandboxProvider implements the same SandboxProvider ABC as DockerSandboxProvider and AzureSandboxProvider, so callers can swap backends without changing application code.

docs.title: Generic SandboxProvider Interface (Recap)
docs.text:
The SandboxProvider ABC and its create_session / execute_code / destroy_session lifecycle are defined in DOCKER-SANDBOX-ISOLATION-DESIGN.md. The returned SessionHandle and ExecutionHandle types, status enums, and async defaults are reused unchanged.

Method HyperLightSandboxProvider behavior create_session(agent_id, policy, config) Constructs a hyperlight_sandbox.Sandbox for the chosen backend (default: Wasm + packaged Python guest), calls register_tool only for names in policy.tool_allowlist, calls allow_domain for each entry in policy.network_allowlist, mounts /input / /output capability directories per config, and stores the sandbox for the session. execute_code(agent_id, session_id, code, *, context) Evaluates the policy, then forwards code to Sandbox.run(code). The guest is responsible for calling call_tool(name, **args) and http_get(url) as needed. Returns stdout/stderr in a SandboxResult. destroy_session(agent_id, session_id) Drops the Sandbox (Python __del__ releases the underlying VM); removes the session entry and the policy evaluator. is_available() True if hyperlight_sandbox imports successfully and a hypervisor (KVM, mshv, or WHP) is reachable. save_state / restore_state / list_checkpoints / delete_checkpoint Not implemented. Hyperlight has no filesystem to commit. Use snapshot_session / restore_snapshot instead — see Snapshots.

Code written against the SandboxProvider ABC works unchanged on Hyperlight. Docker-only methods raise NotImplementedError on this provider; callers that depend on them must check isinstance(provider, DockerSandboxProvider) first.

docs.title: DockerSandboxProvider vs HyperLightSandboxProvider
docs.text: Aspect Docker Hyperlight (hyperlight-sandbox) Isolation Container (runc); optional gVisor / Kata Hardware VM (KVM / mshv / WHP) wrapping a Wasm component, HyperlightJS, or Nanvix guest Kernel Shared with host (or guest kernel for Kata) No general-purpose OS in the guest; the guest sees only the Wasm component runtime / HyperlightJS runtime / Nanvix microkernel Filesystem in guest Full Linux FS (read-only root + tmpfs) Capability-based: read-only /input, writable /output, no other paths Code model python -c "…" in container Sandbox.run("…") runs Python source on the packaged Python guest, or JS source on the JS guest Tool calls HTTP proxy on host port 9100 Sandbox.register_tool(name, callable); guest calls call_tool(name, **args) with schema-validated args, brokered through the hypervisor Network HTTP proxy + iptables (Linux) / proxy-only (macOS, Windows) Sandbox.allow_domain(url); guest calls upstream-provided http_get / fetch. No network at all if no domain allowed. Resource limits cgroups (mem_limit, nano_cpus) Hyperlight SandboxConfiguration (heap, stack, max_execution_time) — set by upstream; AGT exposes the knobs via HyperlightConfig State Persists across exec_run calls; docker commit snapshots Persists across Sandbox.run calls; in-memory snapshot() / restore(snap) (CoW) Cold start 100–500 ms 1–10 ms for empty Wasm guest; ~50 ms for the Python guest's first warmup, then 1–10 ms Memory overhead 50–200 MB per container 1–10 MB for the runtime; the Python guest adds the size of the embedded CPython component Platform Linux native; macOS/Windows via Docker Desktop Linux VM Linux (KVM or mshv); Windows 11 / Server 2022+ (WHP); macOS not supported License of the runtime Apache-2.0 (docker/runc) Apache-2.0 (hyperlight, hyperlight-sandbox)

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
1. Implement SandboxProvider on hyperlight-sandbox so callers can run high-risk agents with hardware-level isolation through the same API as the Docker backend.
  2. Map tool_allowlist directly to register_tool calls. Only host functions named in the allowlist are registered; tools registered with the provider but not allowlisted are not handed to the guest at all.
  3. Map network_allowlist to allow_domain calls. No allowed domains ⇒ guest has zero network capability — http_get / fetch will refuse every URL because the allowlist is empty.
  4. Pass policy defaults.timeout_seconds and resource caps through to Hyperlight's max_execution_time / heap / stack via the upstream Sandbox builder.
  5. Preserve session state across calls within a single Sandbox, matching the Docker provider's "container reused across execute_code calls" model.
  6. Expose snapshots as provider-specific snapshot_session / restore_snapshot methods — not on the ABC.
  7. Support all three upstream backends via a backend= parameter: "wasm" (default; runs Python or JS), "hyperlightjs" (JS-only, smaller footprint), "nanvix" (limited preview — no tools, FS, network, or snapshots).

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
• Running arbitrary shell commands or starting subprocesses inside the sandbox. The packaged Python guest is a sandboxed CPython interpreter; it does not have subprocess, os.system, or a process model. Use tools (register_tool) for anything beyond pure-Python computation.
  • macOS support. Hyperlight requires KVM, mshv, or WHP. macOS users should run AGT in a Linux VM or use the Docker provider.
  • Building or shipping our own guest binaries. Use the packaged Python guest, the packaged JS guest, or — for advanced users — a custom Wasm component, but this provider does not own a guest build.
  • Filesystem checkpointing. The guest's filesystem is /input (read-only) and /output (per-session, ephemeral). Snapshots capture VM memory only.
  • Using the lower-level hyperlight host crate directly. The provider defers to hyperlight-sandbox so AGT rides upstream security fixes and feature work rather than maintaining its own host integration.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
HyperLightSandboxProvider implements the SandboxProvider ABC by adapting upstream hyperlight_sandbox.Sandbox. Tool calls and network access are both delivered as registered capabilities at session-construction time. The guest can only reach a tool if it was passed to register_tool before construction; it can only reach a domain if it was passed to allow_domain before construction. Capabilities the policy does not grant do not exist in the guest's call vocabulary at runtime — the strongest form of policy enforcement available.

Capability Detail Isolation Hardware-virtualized micro-VM per session (KVM / mshv / WHP) Tool enforcement Sandbox.register_tool(name, callable) per policy tool_allowlist; tools registered with the provider but not in the allowlist are not handed to the guest Network enforcement Sandbox.allow_domain(url) per policy network_allowlist; if the allowlist is empty, http_get and fetch refuse every URL Code surface in guest Real Python source (Wasm + packaged Python guest) or JS source (HyperlightJS / Wasm + JS guest); no shell, no subprocess, no filesystem outside /input and /output State In-memory; persists across Sandbox.run calls; optional snapshot() / restore() Session lifecycle One Sandbox per session; session ends when the Python object is dropped Platform support Linux (KVM or mshv); Windows 11 / Server 2022+ (WHP); no macOS

docs.title: Component Overview
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    SandboxProvider (ABC)                      │
│                                                              │
│  create_session(agent_id, policy, config) -> SessionHandle   │
│  execute_code(agent_id, session_id, code, ...) -> ExecHandle │
│  destroy_session(agent_id, session_id)                       │
└──────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
               │
               ▼
    ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │           HyperLightSandboxProvider (Python)            │
    │                                                        │
    │  - Translates SandboxProvider calls into hyperlight-    │
    │    sandbox SDK calls                                   │
    │  - Holds (agent_id, session_id) → hyperlight_sandbox   │
    │    Sandbox instance                                    │
    │  - Holds (agent_id, session_id) → PolicyEvaluator      │
    └─────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┘
                  │  hyperlight_sandbox Python SDK (PyO3)
                  ▼
    ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │       hyperlight_sandbox.Sandbox  (upstream SDK)        │
    │                                                        │
    │  Sandbox(backend="wasm", module="python_guest")        │
    │  .register_tool("web_search", lambda q: ...)           │
    │  .allow_domain("https://api.arxiv.org")                │
    │  .run("import json; print(call_tool('web_search', ...))│
    └─────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┘
                  │  hyperlight host crate (KVM / mshv / WHP)
                  ▼
    ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                Hypervisor (KVM / mshv / WHP)            │
    │                                                        │
    │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
    │  │  Hyperlight micro-VM                             │  │
    │  │                                                  │  │
    │  │  Wasm component runtime (hyperlight-wasm)        │  │
    │  │   └─ Python guest (CPython compiled to Wasm)     │  │
    │  │       ├─ user code from Sandbox.run("…")         │  │
    │  │       ├─ call_tool(name, **kw)  → hypervisor     │  │
    │  │       ├─ http_get(url) / fetch(url)  → host      │  │
    │  │       └─ /input (RO) /output (RW)                │  │
    │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
    └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Crash isolation
docs.text: A guest fault traps to the hypervisor, which returns a Rust error to Sandbox.run, which raises an exception that AGT catches in execute_code. The host Python process is never left in a corrupt state by a misbehaving guest, so the provider does not need an out-of-process runner for fault containment.

docs.title: Backend selection
docs.text:
HyperLightSandboxProvider(backend="wasm",      module="python_guest")  # default
HyperLightSandboxProvider(backend="wasm",      module="javascript_guest")
HyperLightSandboxProvider(backend="hyperlightjs")
HyperLightSandboxProvider(backend="nanvix",    language="python")
Backend Guest languages Tools FS (/input, /output) Network Snapshots When to use wasm (default) Python, JS, custom Wasm ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅ The default for AGT — everything works hyperlightjs JS only ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅ JS-only workloads with smaller footprint nanvix Python or JS ❌ ❌ ❌ ❌ Preview only; raw print is the only side effect — useful for benchmarking and microkernel research

The provider raises ValueError at __init__ if a backend is requested but its upstream extra is not installed (hyperlight-sandbox[wasm,python_guest], hyperlight-sandbox[wasm,js_guest], hyperlight-sandbox[hyperlightjs], hyperlight-sandbox[nanvix]).

docs.title: Code Argument Semantics
docs.text:
SandboxProvider.execute_code(code, *, context) takes a string code argument. On Docker this is Python source executed by python -c. On Hyperlight it is the same — Python source for the wasm + python_guest backend, JavaScript source for the JS backends. The provider forwards code to upstream's Sandbox.run(code) unchanged. With hyperlight-sandbox's packaged Python guest the guest is a Python interpreter, so the natural contract for execute_code matches Docker's and callers do not need to know they are on a different backend.

The context kwarg is passed through Sandbox.run's globals so the guest can read it via globals()["context"]. AGT does not mutate code to inject a context binding — that would change the source the user gave us and confuse policy auditors.

docs.title: Integration with agent-os Policy
docs.text:
PolicyDocument (YAML / runtime object)
  ├── rules[]            → PolicyEvaluator; evaluated on every execute_code
  ├── defaults.max_cpu / max_memory_mb / timeout_seconds
  │                      → HyperlightConfig (heap, stack, max_execution_time)
  ├── tool_allowlist     → Sandbox.register_tool(name, callable) for each
  │                        allowlisted name found in self._tools
  └── network_allowlist  → Sandbox.allow_domain(url) for each entry;
                           empty / absent ⇒ guest has no network capability

When create_session receives a PolicyDocument (or the SimpleNamespace wrapper described in the Azure backend doc):

  1. Resource limits are translated to Hyperlight's SandboxConfiguration knobs by the upstream Sandbox builder.
  2. The provider iterates policy.tool_allowlist. For each name found in self._tools, it calls sandbox.register_tool(name, self._tools[name]). Names listed in the allowlist that are not in self._tools raise ValueError — fail closed at session creation, not call time.
  3. The provider iterates policy.network_allowlist. For each entry it calls sandbox.allow_domain(entry). If the allowlist is empty or missing, no allow_domain calls are made; the guest's http_get refuses every URL.
  4. A PolicyEvaluator is bound to (agent_id, session_id) and re-used on every execute_code call.
from agent_os.policies import PolicyDocument
from agent_sandbox import HyperLightSandboxProvider

policy = PolicyDocument.from_yaml("policies/research_policy.yaml")
provider = HyperLightSandboxProvider(
    backend="wasm",
    module="python_guest",
    tools={
        "web_search": lambda query: search(query),
        "read_doc":   lambda doc_id: load_doc(doc_id),
        # delete_doc is registered with the provider but NOT in the
        # policy's tool_allowlist → it is not handed to the guest
        "delete_doc": lambda doc_id: delete_doc(doc_id),
    },
)

handle = provider.create_session("research-agent", policy=policy)

exec_handle = provider.execute_code(
    "research-agent",
    handle.session_id,
    code=(
        "summary = call_tool('web_search', query='hyperlight')\n"
        "doc     = call_tool('read_doc',   doc_id='2401.12345')\n"
        "abstract = http_get('https://api.arxiv.org/abs/2401.12345')['body']\n"
        "print(summary[:200], len(abstract))\n"
    ),
)
print(exec_handle.result.stdout)

provider.destroy_session("research-agent", handle.session_id)

docs.title: create_session(agent_id, policy, config) → SessionHandle
docs.text:
1. Hypervisor / SDK check. If is_available() is false, raise RuntimeError("hyperlight-sandbox unavailable: install the SDK and " "ensure a hypervisor (KVM / mshv / WHP) is reachable").
  2. session_id = uuid4().hex[:8].
  3. Resolve resource caps from policy.defaults (max_cpu is ignored on Hyperlight — one vCPU per micro-VM; max_memory_mb / timeout_seconds become heap-budget and max_execution_time).
  4. Resolve tool_allowlist. allowed = {n: self._tools[n] for n in policy.tool_allowlist if n in self._tools}. Missing names raise ValueError.
  5. Resolve network_allowlist. Capture the list verbatim. An empty list disables network entirely (no allow_domain calls).
  6. Construct the upstream Sandbox.
sandbox = hyperlight_sandbox.Sandbox(
    backend=self._backend,
    module=self._module,
    max_execution_time_ms=cfg.timeout_seconds * 1000,
    heap_size_bytes=cfg.max_memory_mb * 1024 * 1024,
)
for name, fn in allowed.items():
    sandbox.register_tool(name, fn)
for url in net_allow:
    sandbox.allow_domain(url)
  1. Bind a PolicyEvaluator. self._evaluators[(agent_id, session_id)] = PolicyEvaluator(policies=[policy]).
  2. Return SessionHandle(agent_id, session_id, status=READY).

docs.title: execute_code(agent_id, session_id, code, *, context) → ExecutionHandle
docs.text:
1. Session lookup. (agent_id, session_id) must be in _sandboxes, otherwise RuntimeError("No active session — call create_session() first").
  2. Policy gate. Evaluate the bound PolicyEvaluator. On deny, raise PermissionError before any guest call.
  3. Run the guest.
try:
    result = sandbox.run(code, globals={"context": context or {}})
except hyperlight_sandbox.GuestTrap as exc:
    return ExecutionHandle(... status=FAILED,
        result=SandboxResult(success=False, exit_code=1,
                             stderr=str(exc), ...))

Sandbox.run returns an object with stdout, stderr, and duration_ms attributes. We wrap them in SandboxResult exactly like the Docker provider. 4. Wall-clock kill. If result.duration_ms / 1000 > cfg.timeout_seconds, set result.killed = True. The hypervisor already tore down the call — this only flags it for the caller. 5. Return ExecutionHandle(..., status=COMPLETED|FAILED, result=...).

docs.title: destroy_session(agent_id, session_id) → None
docs.text:
1. Pop the Sandbox and the PolicyEvaluator from the bookkeeping dicts.
  2. Drop the Sandbox reference; upstream releases the underlying VM.
  3. Idempotent — calling on an unknown session is a no-op (matches the Azure provider).

docs.title: is_available() → bool
docs.text:
True iff:

  • import hyperlight_sandbox succeeds, and
  • hyperlight_sandbox.is_hypervisor_present() returns True (KVM, mshv, or WHP reachable), and
  • the requested backend's optional dependency is installed (e.g. the packaged Python guest for backend="wasm", module="python_guest").

docs.title: Snapshots (provider-specific, not on the ABC)
docs.text:
snap = provider.snapshot_session("research-agent", session_id)
# … run more code, possibly diverge …
provider.restore_snapshot("research-agent", session_id, snap.snapshot_id)

snapshot_session calls Sandbox.snapshot(); restore_snapshot calls Sandbox.restore(snap). Snapshots live in process memory; destroying the session drops them.

docs.title: Tool-Call Restriction (Host Functions)
docs.text: Hyperlight's call mechanism makes the policy's tool_allowlist an integral part of how a tool becomes callable, not a check applied later.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Hyperlight Guest VM (Wasm + Python guest)        │
│                                                  │
│  user code:                                      │
│    summary = call_tool('web_search',             │
│                        query='hyperlight')       │
│         │                                        │
│         │ hypercall (flatbuffer payload)         │
│         ▼                                        │
└─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┘
          │
          ▼  (hypervisor-mediated call_tool dispatch)
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  hyperlight-sandbox host bindings                 │
│                                                  │
│  Tools registered for THIS session (per policy): │
│    web_search(query) -> bytes                    │
│    read_doc(doc_id) -> bytes                     │
│                                                  │
│  NOT registered:                                 │
│    delete_doc(...)   ← uncallable                │
│    fs_read(path)     ← uncallable                │
│    spawn_process(..) ← uncallable                │
└─────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
          │  Python callback into self._tools[name]
          ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  HyperLightSandboxProvider                       │
│  Returns the Python callable's result            │
│  (schema-validated by upstream before dispatch)  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Why this is stronger than the Docker ToolCallProxy
docs.text:
Property Docker ToolCallProxy Hyperlight register_tool Where the allowlist is enforced HTTP server on host port 9100 Hypervisor call gate, decided at Sandbox construction What happens if the agent ignores the tool API Could attempt raw sockets (blocked by iptables on Linux; bypassable on macOS/Windows) The agent has no symbol to call. There is no "raw sockets" alternative — there is no kernel and no syscall. Allowlist mutation at runtime proxy.register(ip, allowlist) — a bug in the proxy could grant unintended tools Tool set is fixed at construction; not mutable for the life of the session Trusted computing base ToolCallProxy (Python), HTTP parser, iptables Hyperlight host crate, hypervisor

A session created without a tool_allowlist registers no tools. The guest can still execute code — print, arithmetic, the standard library — but it has no way to reach Python callables on the host.

docs.title: Network Domain Restriction
docs.text: Hyperlight guests have no general-purpose network stack. Network egress can only occur through http_get / fetch, which upstream registers automatically and which validates every URL against the allowlist passed to allow_domain.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Hyperlight Guest VM (no socket syscalls)         │
│                                                  │
│  resp = http_get(                                │
│      "https://api.arxiv.org/abs/2401.12345")     │
│       │                                          │
│       │ hypercall                                │
│       ▼                                          │
└───────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  hyperlight-sandbox http_get implementation       │
│                                                  │
│  1. Parse url → "api.arxiv.org"                  │
│  2. Match against this Sandbox's allow list      │
│     (subdomain rule applied by upstream)         │
│     ├─ deny  → return {status: 403, ...}         │
│     └─ allow → reqwest::Client::get(url)         │
│  3. Stream response body back as bytes           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Allowlist rules (handled by upstream)
docs.text: Rule Behavior Exact match allow_domain("https://api.arxiv.org") allows requests to that exact host Subdomain upstream's matcher accepts subdomain expansions (e.g. arxiv.org allows api.arxiv.org) Scheme https:// is the default; http:// requires an explicit allow_domain("http://…") entry Method All standard HTTP methods are supported; the policy can restrict via tool wrappers if tighter control is needed Body / header sizes Bounded by Hyperlight's input/output buffer config

docs.title: Why this is un-bypassable
docs.text:
Bypass technique on Docker Status on Hyperlight Unset HTTP_PROXY env var Irrelevant — there is no proxy and no env-driven HTTP client in the guest Open a raw TCP socket Irrelevant — there is no socket syscall and no kernel curl directly to an IP Irrelevant — there is no curl, no shell, no process model DNS lookup to internal IP Irrelevant — the host parses and validates the URL before any DNS happens Spawn a child process Irrelevant — guest cannot create processes

Network policy bypass on Hyperlight requires either a hyperlight-sandbox host-side vulnerability or a hypervisor escape (KVM / mshv / WHP). Both are escalations of severity an order of magnitude beyond a typical Docker bypass.

A session created without a network_allowlist makes zero allow_domain calls. The guest can still call http_get, but every URL is denied at the host before any network I/O happens.

docs.title: Upstream APIs Used
docs.text: Step hyperlight_sandbox API Purpose Probe runtime is_hypervisor_present() Detect KVM / mshv / WHP availability Construct sandbox Sandbox(backend=…, module=…) Build a per-session VM Register tool sandbox.register_tool(name, callable) One call per allowlisted tool Allow domain sandbox.allow_domain(url) One call per network_allowlist entry Mount input dir sandbox.mount_input(path) Read-only /input Mount output dir sandbox.mount_output(path) Per-session writable /output Run code sandbox.run(source) -> RunResult Execute Python (or JS) source Snapshot sandbox.snapshot() -> Snapshot Capture VM memory (CoW) Restore sandbox.restore(snapshot) Restore VM memory Cancel sandbox.interrupt() Trigger max_execution_time termination

docs.title: HyperlightConfig Mapping
docs.text:
@dataclass
class HyperlightConfig:
    backend: Literal["wasm", "hyperlightjs", "nanvix"] = "wasm"
    module:  str = "python_guest"        # ignored for hyperlightjs/nanvix
    heap_size_bytes:        int = 64 * 1024 * 1024
    stack_size_bytes:       int = 2  * 1024 * 1024
    max_execution_time_ms:  int = 60_000
    input_dir:  Path | None = None       # mounted read-only as /input
    output_dir: Path | None = None       # mounted writable as /output

max_execution_time_ms enforces a per-call timeout at the hypervisor boundary — the guest is unconditionally torn out of execution when it expires. This is the analogue of the Docker provider's per-call timeout, but enforced by the hypervisor rather than by the SDK.

docs.title: Internal Data Structures
docs.text:
HyperLightSandboxProvider
├── _backend: Literal["wasm","hyperlightjs","nanvix"]
├── _module: str | None
├── _tools: dict[str, Callable]                            # provider-registered callables
├── _available: bool                                       # hypervisor + SDK detected at __init__
├── _sandboxes: dict[tuple[str,str], hyperlight_sandbox.Sandbox]
├── _evaluators: dict[tuple[str,str], PolicyEvaluator]
└── _state_lock: threading.Lock

All guest dispatch happens in-process through the upstream Python SDK, so the provider does not need its own runner subprocess, host-call thread, or RPC framing — _sandboxes and _evaluators are the entire bookkeeping surface.

docs.title: Request Flow: execute_code() End-to-End
docs.text:
caller
  │
  ▼
provider.execute_code_async(agent_id="a1", session_id="abc12345",
                            code="…python source…",
                            context={...})
  │
  ├─ 0. Session check
  │     └─ ("a1", "abc12345") not in _sandboxes
  │        → raise RuntimeError("No active session — call create_session() first")
  │
  ├─ 1. Policy gate (host-side)
  │     ├─ _evaluators[("a1","abc12345")] exists
  │     │  → evaluator.evaluate({"agent_id":"a1","action":"execute","code":code,...})
  │     │     ├─ denied  → raise PermissionError  (no guest traffic)
  │     │     └─ allowed → continue
  │     └─ No evaluator → skip
  │
  ├─ 2. sandbox.run(code, globals={"context": context or {}})
  │     ├─ guest may call call_tool(name, **kw) → upstream dispatches
  │     │   to self._tools[name] iff registered
  │     ├─ guest may call http_get(url)        → upstream allows iff
  │     │   url.host ∈ allow_domain set
  │     ├─ guest may write to /output (if mounted)
  │     └─ returns RunResult(stdout, stderr, duration_ms, exit_code)
  │
  └─ 3. Wrap in ExecutionHandle / SandboxResult
        ├─ honor cfg.timeout_seconds (set killed=True if exceeded)
        └─ truncate stdout/stderr to 10 KiB

docs.title: Comparison to the Azure Sandbox provider
docs.text:
HyperLightSandboxProvider and AzureSandboxProvider solve different points in the deployment matrix:

Concern Azure Hyperlight Where the sandbox runs Azure Container Apps (managed) The agent's host machine Who provisions the VM Microsoft The agent runtime Cold-start cost seconds (Azure provisioning) milliseconds Steady-state latency ~100 ms (data-plane round trip) microseconds (in-process) Best for Multi-tenant production agents and bursty workloads Local dev, edge, on-prem, latency-sensitive control loops Network policy enforcement Azure egress proxy (HTTP 403) Upstream allow_domain (host-side reject) Tool isolation None — host-side PolicyEvaluator only Hypervisor call gate

Both implement the same SandboxProvider ABC, so an application can switch between them by changing the constructor.

docs.title: Risks & open questions
docs.text:
• Upstream API stability. hyperlight-sandbox is at v0.4.0 (CNCF Sandbox project). The API is moving but the surface used here (register_tool, allow_domain, run, snapshot/restore) has been stable across the v0.3 → v0.4 jump. We should pin a tight version range and bump deliberately, mirroring our policy for azure-sandbox.
  • Wasm Python guest size. The packaged Python guest carries a CPython build inside the Wasm component. First-run materialization can be tens of MiB. This is a one-time cost, but worth documenting for air-gapped environments.
  • Tool argument schemas. Upstream validates tool args against a schema derived from the registered Python callable's type hints. Untyped callables fall back to a permissive schema. AGT should require type hints (or explicit inspect.signature(...) decoration) for any tool that crosses the trust boundary.
  • context propagation. Passing context via globals() is a pragmatic choice; if upstream adds first-class context support to Sandbox.run we should switch.
  • macOS. Still no path. Document the gap and suggest WSL or remote Linux dev for macOS-based contributors.
  • Nanvix backend. Currently a preview with no tools, no FS, no network, no snapshots. Useful for benchmarking the microkernel path, but not appropriate for production. The provider exposes it for completeness and refuses to register tools or apply a network allowlist on a Nanvix session.

docs.title: Out of scope (deferred to follow-up designs)
docs.text:
• Custom guest binaries beyond the upstream Python and JS packages.
  • Multi-tenant pooling (one sandbox shared across agents). Today every session is its own VM; pooling is a separate trust analysis.
  • Cross-backend feature parity. We accept that nanvix is partial; the ABC's is_available() should expose this.
  • Telemetry hooks. Upstream does not yet emit structured per-call metrics. When it does, AGT should map them into the existing agent-sre observability surface.

docs.text: Submission: lfai/proposing-projects#102 Status: Open — awaiting TAC review Requested Level: Sandbox Date Submitted: March 2, 2026

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Agent Governance Toolkit is an open-source governance kernel for autonomous AI agents providing runtime policy enforcement, capability sandboxing, inter-agent trust verification, and kill-switch controls.

The ecosystem consists of 5 interoperating packages:

Package Purpose PyPI Agent OS Core governance kernel (policy engine, capability sandbox, VFS) agent-os Agent Mesh Inter-agent trust layer (DID identity, IATP protocol) agent-mesh Agent Runtime Execution isolation (ring model, kill switch) agentmesh-runtime Agent SRE Observability & reliability (circuit breakers, anomaly detection) agent-sre Agent Governance Meta-framework & compliance mapping agent-governance

docs.title: Alignment with LF AI & Data Mission
docs.text: As AI agents become increasingly autonomous, governance infrastructure is critical for safe deployment. The Agent Governance Toolkit provides this as a neutral, open-source project — preventing vendor lock-in and enabling a shared governance standard.

docs.title: Why LF AI & Data?

1. Neutral governance home — The toolkit needs a vendor-neutral foundation as it moves from Microsoft personal repos to a community project
  2. Cross-framework — Integrations with LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, Google ADK, PydanticAI, Mastra, OpenAI Agents SDK, and Microsoft Agent Framework
  3. Standards alignment — Active proposals at AAIF, CoSAI/OASIS WS4, and OWASP

docs.title: Overview

docs.text: Governance needs to work across all MCP clients (Claude, Copilot, ADK, Cursor, etc.) without each client implementing their own governance logic. A centralized MCP governance server provides universal policy enforcement.

docs.title: MetaGPT — Trust Layer Integration
docs.text: Submission: FoundationAgents/MetaGPT#1936 Status: Open PR — awaiting review Type: Extension module (metagpt.ext.agentmesh) Date Submitted: March 2, 2026

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Inter-agent trust verification for MetaGPT multi-agent teams using Agent-Mesh. Adds cryptographic identity (DIDs), trust scoring, capability-based access control, and audit logging to MetaGPT's role-based architecture.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
MetaGPT enables powerful multi-agent collaboration through specialized roles, but agents interact without verifying trust: - ProductManager sends requirements → Is PM authorized? - Architect designs system → Can we trust the design? - Engineer writes code → Should we execute untrusted code?

Without trust verification, a compromised role in the pipeline can inject malicious instructions that propagate through the entire team.

docs.title: TrustedTeam
docs.text:
from metagpt.ext.agentmesh import TrustedTeam, TrustPolicy, TrustLevel

policy = TrustPolicy(
    min_trust_level=TrustLevel.MEDIUM,
    sensitive_actions={"WriteCode", "ExecuteCode"},
    sensitive_action_trust=TrustLevel.HIGH,
)

team = TrustedTeam(policy=policy)
team.add_role(ProductManager(), trust_level=TrustLevel.HIGH)
team.add_role(Engineer(), trust_level=TrustLevel.MEDIUM)

# Verifies trust before interaction
team.verify_message("ProductManager", "Engineer", "AssignTask")

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text: Component Purpose TrustedRole Wraps MetaGPT roles with cryptographic identity TrustPolicy Configurable trust requirements per team TrustVerifier Verifies interactions between agents TrustedTeam Team wrapper with enforcement

docs.title: Trust Levels
docs.text: Level Score Use Case LOW 0.0–0.3 Read-only roles, observers MEDIUM 0.3–0.7 Standard collaboration roles HIGH 0.7–1.0 Code execution, system modifications

docs.title: Value Proposition
docs.text: Feature Without Trust With Agent-Mesh Agent Identity None Cryptographic DIDs Interaction Control None Policy-based Sensitive Actions Unrestricted Trust-gated Audit Trail None Full logging

docs.title: OpenAI Swarm — Trust-Verified Handoffs
docs.text: Submission: openai/swarm#65 Status: Open PR — awaiting review Type: Contrib module (swarm.contrib.agentmesh) Date Submitted: March 2, 2026

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Trust-verified handoffs for OpenAI Swarm using Agent-Mesh's CMVK identity layer. Ensures only trusted agents can receive handoffs, prevents context exfiltration, and maintains a full audit trail of handoff chains.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Swarm enables multi-agent orchestration through handoffs, but has no built-in way to: - Verify the receiving agent is trusted - Prevent handoffs to malicious/compromised agents - Audit handoff chains for compliance - Protect sensitive context during handoffs

In multi-agent systems, handoffs create attack vectors — a compromised agent could intercept sensitive data flowing through the handoff chain.

docs.title: TrustedSwarm
docs.text:
Wrapper around Swarm with trust-verified handoffs: 

from swarm.contrib.agentmesh import TrustedSwarm, TrustPolicy

policy = TrustPolicy(min_trust_score=0.5, audit_logging=True)
swarm = TrustedSwarm(policy=policy)

swarm.register_agent(triage, trust_score=0.8)
swarm.register_agent(sales, trust_score=0.7)

response = swarm.run(triage, messages)  # Handoffs verified

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text: Component Purpose TrustedSwarm Wrapper with trust-verified handoffs TrustPolicy Configurable trust requirements HandoffVerifier Validates trust before allowing handoffs AgentIdentity DID-based agent identification (did:swarm:xxx)

docs.title: Trust Verification Rules
docs.text: Handoffs are blocked when: - Target agent is not registered - Target agent is in blocked list - Target agent's trust score is below threshold - Sensitive context present + insufficient trust level

docs.title: Features
docs.text: Feature Description Trust scores 0.0–1.0 score per agent Blocked agents Prevent handoffs to specific agents Allowed list Only allow handoffs to approved agents Sensitive context Higher trust required for sensitive data Audit logging Full handoff history with timestamps Violation callbacks Custom handling for blocked handoffs

docs.title: OpenLit — Agent SRE OTel Instrumentation
docs.text: Issue: openlit/openlit#1003 PR: openlit/openlit#1037 Status: ✅ Implemented — Auto-instrumentation complete. Upstream PR #1062 under review. Integration docs PR in progress. Type: New instrumentation (auto-instrumentation via openlit.init()) Date Submitted: March 2, 2026

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Auto-instrumentation for Agent SRE, an AI-native SRE framework (1,071+ tests), that captures SLI/SLO tracking, chaos testing, and error budget operations as OpenTelemetry spans and metrics in OpenLit.

docs.title: Integration Rationale
docs.text: OpenLit is OpenTelemetry-native with 50+ LLM provider integrations. Agent SRE complements this by adding the SRE discipline layer — SLO health, chaos resilience, and error budget tracking alongside LLM traces.

docs.title: What's Instrumented
docs.text: Operation Span Name Key Attributes SLO.evaluate() slo.evaluate {name} status, error_budget_remaining, burn_rate, is_exhausted ErrorBudget.record_event() error_budget.record {good\|bad} total, consumed, remaining_percent ChaosExperiment.start() chaos.start {name} target_agent, fault_count, blast_radius ChaosExperiment.complete() chaos.complete {name} resilience_score, resilience_passed, duration ChaosExperiment.abort() chaos.abort {name} abort_reason, injection_count SLI.record() (detailed) sli.record {name} value, target, window

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Since Agent SRE already exports native OTel metrics and traces, the instrumentation wraps core methods using wrapt (same pattern as CrewAI, LangChain, etc.) to automatically capture SRE telemetry when openlit.init() is called.

No OpenLit-specific code needed in agent-sre — the instrumentation monkey-patches at import time.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose instrumentation/agent_sre/__init__.py AgentSREInstrumentor class (BaseInstrumentor) instrumentation/agent_sre/agent_sre.py 5 sync wrappers for all operations instrumentation/agent_sre/utils.py Span attribute helpers + metrics recording _instrumentors.py Registered agent-sre in MODULE_NAME_MAP + INSTRUMENTOR_MAP

docs.title: OTel Conventions
docs.text: All metrics are prefixed agent.sre.*: - agent.sre.sli.value — SLI measurement value - agent.sre.burn_rate — SLO burn rate - agent.sre.chaos.resilience_score — Chaos experiment resilience score - agent.sre.chaos.experiment_id — Experiment identifier

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
With this instrumentation, OpenLit users running agent-sre can see:

  1. SLO Health Dashboards — Real-time error budget burn rate, SLI compliance across agent systems
  2. Chaos Experiment Traces — Fault injection events, resilience scores, abort conditions alongside LLM calls they affect
  3. Error Budget Consumption — Track good/bad events against budget to decide when to deploy new features vs focus on reliability
  4. Canary Metrics — During progressive rollouts of new LLM models/prompts, track canary vs baseline performance

docs.title: Companion Integration
docs.text:
The toolkit also includes an OpenLitExporter convenience class in Agent SRE that pre-configures OTel SDK for OpenLit's OTLP endpoint:

from agent_sre.integrations.openlit import OpenLitExporter

exporter = OpenLitExporter(
    endpoint="http://localhost:4318",
    service_name="my-ai-service"
)
exporter.record_slo("latency-p99", slo_result)
exporter.record_chaos_experiment("failover-test", experiment)

docs.title: OWASP Agent Security Initiative — Code Samples Contribution
docs.text: Submission: GenAI-Security-Project/GenAI-Agent-Security-Initiative#2 Status: Open — awaiting review Type: Pull Request (insecure/secure code pairs) Date Submitted: March 2, 2026

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Contribution of insecure/secure code pairs to the OWASP Agent Security Initiative (ASI), demonstrating how the Agent Governance Toolkit mitigates OWASP Agentic Top 10 risks. Initial submission covers 3 risks with plans to expand to all 10 covered risks.

docs.title: ASI-01: Agent Hijacking
docs.text: Description Vulnerability Unrestricted goal modification — agent accepts arbitrary goals without validation Insecure Agent directly executes user-provided goals with no policy check Secure PolicyEngine validates goals against declarative YAML policy before execution Package Agent OS (agent_os.PolicyEngine)

docs.title: ASI-02: Excessive Capabilities
docs.text: Description Vulnerability Unrestricted filesystem and network access — agent has full system access Insecure Agent directly calls os.system(), open(), requests.get() without restrictions Secure CapabilitySandbox enforces ring-based least-privilege with explicit capability grants Package Agent OS (agent_os.CapabilitySandbox)

docs.title: ASI-05: Insecure Output
docs.text: Description Vulnerability Raw agent output passed directly to SQL queries — SQL injection risk Insecure Agent output used in f-string SQL query without sanitization Secure OutputValidator from Agent Runtime sanitizes output before downstream consumption Package Agent Runtime (hypervisor.OutputValidator)

docs.title: RAG Governance — Retrieval-Level Policy Enforcement
docs.text:
Submissions:

  • microsoft/agent-governance-toolkit#1700 — RAG governance package proposal (Open Issue)

Type: New package (agent-rag-governance)

Date Submitted: May 4, 2026

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Retrieval-level governance for RAG pipelines using Agent-OS. Adds collection-level access control, rate limiting, content scanning, and audit trails before retrieved chunks reach the LLM. The core RAGGovernor works with any retriever — framework-specific wrappers for LangChain and LlamaIndex are provided as separate adapters, with room for more frameworks to be added without changing the core.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
RAG pipelines lack built-in policy enforcement at retrieval time:

  • Agents can query any vector DB collection without restriction
  • No audit trail of which documents influenced an answer
  • No rate limiting if an agent gets stuck in a retrieval loop
  • No content scanning on retrieved chunks before they reach the LLM

docs.title: RAGGovernor
docs.text:
from agent_rag_governance import RAGGovernor
from agent_rag_governance.policies import CollectionACL, ContentPolicy

governor = RAGGovernor(
    acl=CollectionACL(
        allowed=["public_docs", "product_manuals"],
        denied=["hr_records", "financial_data"],
    ),
    rate_limit=100,
    content_policies=[ContentPolicy.BLOCK_PII, ContentPolicy.BLOCK_INJECTIONS],
    audit_enabled=True,
)

docs.title: Framework Adapters
docs.text:
Framework-specific wrappers over the core RAGGovernor, kept separate from the core so new frameworks can be added without touching RAGGovernor:

# LangChain adapter
from agent_rag_governance.adapters.langchain import GovernedRetriever

governed_retriever = GovernedRetriever(
    retriever=your_langchain_retriever,
    governor=governor,
)
docs = governed_retriever.get_relevant_documents("your query")
print(f"Blocked retrievals: {governed_retriever.violations}")

# LlamaIndex adapter
from agent_rag_governance.adapters.llamaindex import GovernedQueryEngine

governed_engine = GovernedQueryEngine(
    query_engine=your_llama_query_engine,
    governor=governor,
)

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Collection ACL — Allow/deny vector DB collections per agent identity using existing Cedar/Rego PolicyEvaluator
  • Rate limiting — Cap retrieval calls per minute to prevent runaway agent loops
  • Content scanning — PII detection and injection pattern scanning on retrieved chunks before they reach the LLM
  • Audit trail — Full logging of every retrieval call with timestamp, agent ID, collection, document IDs, and policy decision
  • Framework-agnostic core — Works with any retriever that returns documents; LangChain and LlamaIndex as first adapters. Future contributors can add adapters for CrewAI, AutoGen, Haystack, and others without touching the core RAGGovernor — following the same pattern already used in agentmesh-integrations

docs.title: API Surface
docs.text:
The public API has three entry points:

RAGGovernor — core engine, framework-agnostic:

governor.retrieve(
    query: str,
    collection: str,
    agent_id: str,
    retriever_fn: Callable,
) -> GovernedRetrievalResult

GovernedRetrievalResult — returned from every retrieval call:

@dataclass
class GovernedRetrievalResult:
    allowed: bool             # whether retrieval was permitted
    documents: list[Document] # retrieved chunks (empty if blocked)
    reason: str | None        # policy decision reason
    audit_id: str             # unique ID for this retrieval call

Framework adapters — thin wrappers over the core:

# LangChain
GovernedRetriever(retriever, governor) -> BaseRetriever

# LlamaIndex
GovernedQueryEngine(query_engine, governor) -> BaseQueryEngine

docs.title: Where it sits in the AGT package tree
docs.text:
agent-governance-python/
├── agent-os/               # Policy engine (PolicyEvaluator)
├── agent-mesh/             # Zero-trust identity
├── agent-mcp-governance/   # MCP tool governance
├── agent-rag-governance/   # NEW — retrieval governance
│   ├── src/
│   │   └── agent_rag_governance/
│   │       ├── __init__.py       # Public exports
│   │       ├── governor.py       # RAGGovernor core
│   │       ├── policies.py       # CollectionACL, ContentPolicy
│   │       ├── audit.py          # Audit trail logging
│   │       └── adapters/
│   │           ├── langchain.py  # GovernedRetriever (Phase 1)
│   │           ├── llamaindex.py # GovernedQueryEngine (Phase 1)
│   │           └── ...           # CrewAI, AutoGen, Haystack (future)
│   ├── tests/
│   ├── pyproject.toml
│   └── README.md
└── agent-sre/              # SLOs, circuit breakers

agent-rag-governance sits alongside agent-mcp-governance — both follow the same wrapper pattern, governing a specific action category (MCP tool calls vs retrieval calls) using the shared agent-os policy engine.

docs.title: How it interacts with existing PolicyEvaluator
docs.text:
RAGGovernor delegates all policy decisions to the existing PolicyEvaluator from agent-os — no parallel policy system. Collection ACLs are expressed in Cedar/Rego and evaluated through the same PEP/PDP pair used everywhere else in AGT:

from agent_os.policies import PolicyEvaluator
from agent_rag_governance import RAGGovernor

# Reuse existing PolicyEvaluator
evaluator = PolicyEvaluator(policies=[your_cedar_policy])

governor = RAGGovernor(
    policy_evaluator=evaluator,  # plugs in directly
    rate_limit=100,
    audit_enabled=True,
)

Cedar policy example for collection ACL:

permit(
  principal == Agent::"my-agent",
  action == Action::"retrieve",
  resource in Collection::"public_docs"
);

forbid(
  principal,
  action == Action::"retrieve",
  resource in Collection::"hr_records"
);

The RAGGovernor calls evaluator.evaluate() before every retrieval — the same call pattern used by agent-mcp-governance and agent-os tool governance. This means existing policies can cover both tool calls and retrieval calls in one policy file.

docs.title: Value Proposition
docs.text: Feature Without Package With agent-rag-governance Collection access control None Cedar/Rego per agent identity Rate limiting None Configurable per agent Content scanning None PII + injection detection Audit trail None Full per-retrieval log Framework support N/A LangChain + LlamaIndex

docs.title: Example PR
docs.text:
End-to-end example demonstrating agent-rag-governance in action:

  • RAGGovernor setup with CollectionACL and ContentPolicy
  • Cedar/Rego policy configuration for collection access control
  • Framework-agnostic retrieval with governance enforcement
  • Rate limiting triggering on a runaway retrieval loop
  • PII detection blocking a chunk before it reaches the LLM
  • Audit trail output showing timestamp, agent ID, collection, document IDs, and policy decision per retrieval call

docs.title: Proposal: Stripe Machine Payments Protocol Integration
docs.text: Status: 📋 Planned — Research complete, implementation not started Author: Agent Governance Toolkit Team (Microsoft) Created: 2026-03-21

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Integrate Stripe's Machine Payments Protocol (MPP) with AGT to enable governed, autonomous agent-to-agent payments with spending delegation, escrow verification, and audit trails.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Stripe launched MPP in March 2026 — an open standard for agent-initiated payments supporting fiat + stablecoin. The "Sessions" primitive provides pre-authorized spending caps (OAuth for money).

docs.title: Proposed Integration
docs.text:
1. Agent Wallet — VADP delegation receipt with spending caps maps to Stripe MPP sessions
  2. Escrow Bridge — Connect Nexus escrow system to Stripe payment confirmation
  3. Compliance — EU PSD3 SCA satisfied at delegation time; agents transact within authorized scope
  4. Audit Trail — Every payment linked to VADP delegation chain for provenance

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Human → VADP Delegation (spend ≤ $X) → Agent Wallet → Stripe MPP Session → Payment
                                                    ↕
                                              Nexus Escrow
                                           (credits locked until confirmation)

docs.text:
